content.assessment: Migrated lens reading for 'Structural delay of the selection age (Finnish/Polish model)'. Because early selection is codified as a narrow, separately-justified legal exception rather than the structural default of secondary education, reform of the selection age is legally more tractable than reshaping Hungary's broader maintainer or curriculum architecture — but legitimacy and constitutional doctrine require any change to proceed on a felmenő-rendszer (grade-by-grade phase-in) basis with minimum ~1-year statutory lead time, protecting students already admitted under existing rules.
opportunities: Potential achievement gains in the range of Poland's observed 0.16-0.28 SD across subjects, with the largest gains for at-risk/vocational-track-bound students, if Hungary's context proves sufficiently comparable.

Reduced intergenerational income elasticity, by analogy with Finland's observed 23% reduction (0.30 to 0.23) following its delay of tracking from 11 to 16, though Finland's effect was bundled with welfare-state expansion and teacher-education reform not isolated from the tracking-age effect itself.
strengths: Hungarian law explicitly allows gymnasium (4, 6 or 8-grade forms) but treats the 6/8-year track as a distinct legal category requiring 'talent-nurturing' (tehetséggondozás) justification with separately regulated elevated standards, making selection age a codified exception rather than the structural default.

The Nkt. § 4 defines secondary education (középfokú nevelés-oktatás szakasza) as beginning at grade 9, with the 6- and 8-year gymnasium explicitly listed as the only statutory exception to this age boundary.
threats: The scenario identifies a substantial reversal risk citing the Polish precedent (reform reversed after 18 years despite documented gains), and the only entrenchment device contemplated (a cross-party political pact) is not specified with statutory safeguards, independent monitoring triggers, or supermajority requirements, leaving the reform structurally exposed in the same way Poland's was.

Fidesz's immediate framing of any 'unified model' as totalitarian ('no example since communism since') establishes this scenario as the highest-visibility target for culture-war opposition among all five, given it most closely matches the minister's initially floated (then retracted) unified four-year model idea.
weaknesses: Whether Hungary's Constitutional Court (Alkotmánybíróság) would treat abolition or significant raising of the 6/8-year selection age as a matter binding the felmenő-rendszer safeguard as mandatory constitutional doctrine, or whether it would classify it as a discretionary legislative choice subject only to rule-of-law process review.

Whether non-state maintainers (churches, foundations under remaining autonomy frameworks) would face differential legal constraints or political veto power in a national selection-age reform, and whether consensus-building with maintainer federations would constitute a hard prerequisite or a discretionary negotiation.
